channel.title: Whamcloud Community JIRA
channel.description: This file is an XML representation of an issue

item.title: [LU-18710] mmap traffic isn't reflected in the llite stats file
item.description:
NVIDIA Nsight Systems is a system wide profiler. It reads performance counters coming from CPUs, GPUs, NICs, storage volumes, etc. and brings all data to a unified timeline. This helps software developers see how their application executes over a server, to be able to optimize the application's performance.

Additional details are at:

  • https://developer.nvidia.com/nsight-systems 
  • https://docs.nvidia.com/nsight-systems/UserGuide/index.html 

Nsight Systems reads Lustre client performance counters from /sys/kernel/debug/lustre/llite/<volume name>/stats

However, when data is read/written to a file mapped to memory using mmap, the traffic isn't reflected in the stats file.
item.summary: mmap traffic isn't reflected in the llite stats file

status.description: The issue is open and ready for the assignee to start work on it.

comments.comment:
bobijam would you be able to carry out the changes suggested by Andreas?

Would it make sense to increment the read_bytes stats counter by PAGE_SIZE whenever the page_fault counter is incremented?

"Would it make sense to increment the read_bytes stats counter by PAGE_SIZE whenever the page_fault counter is incremented?"

If the Lustre driver brings the entire page from storage, this makes sense to me.
Would it make sense to increment the read_bytes stats counter by PAGE_SIZE whenever the page_fault counter is incremented?

Maybe we still need to account the read-ahead pages (ll_readpage())for page fault()?

we can account it if the I/O is from page fault() context (via LCC_MMAP?)

I ran a quick test with executing a binary from Lustre and it appears that the stats were counted:

# lctl set_param -n llite.*.stats=clear
# lctl set_param -n ldlm.namespaces.*.lru_size=clear
# /myth/tmp/sleep  1
# lctl get_param llite.*.stats 
llite.myth-ffff8d2382e31000.stats=
snapshot_time             1741071528.762256220 secs.nsecs
start_time                1741071501.407516798 secs.nsecs
elapsed_time              27.354739422 secs.nsecs
read_bytes                3 samples [bytes] 28 448 732 267024
read                      3 samples [usecs] 1 226 230 51086
open                      1 samples [usecs] 3 3 3 9
mmap                      2 samples [usecs] 5 10 15 125
page_fault                5 samples [usecs] 0 2 4 8
# ls -l /myth/tmp/sleep
28 -rwxr-xr-x. 1 root 28544 Aug 14  2021 /myth/tmp/sleep

So it is recording the mmap and read operations, but it looks like read_bytes count is too small for the size of the binary.

I also ran our dedicated mmap_read test binary to read a 1GB file, and it is definitely not accounting the bytes read via mmap:

# lustre/tests/mmap_cat /myth/tmp/1G | wc -c
1073741824
# lctl get_param llite.*.stats 
llite.myth-ffff8d2382e31000.stats=
snapshot_time             1741071924.349030965 secs.nsecs
start_time                1741071501.407516798 secs.nsecs
elapsed_time              422.941514167 secs.nsecs
read_bytes                6 samples [bytes] 28 448 1464 534048
read                      6 samples [usecs] 1 226 240 51152
open                      3 samples [usecs] 3 314 488 127846
close                     3 samples [usecs] 110 329 571 137765
mmap                      5 samples [usecs] 5 12 43 415
page_fault                262154 samples [usecs] 0 34 2198 12352

but it is accounting this in the page_fault counter (262144 x 4096-byte pages).

We do have stats for mmap operations.

 { LPROC_LL_MMAP,        LPROCFS_TYPE_LATENCY,   "mmap" },
 { LPROC_LL_FAULT,       LPROCFS_TYPE_LATENCY,   "page_fault" },
  { LPROC_LL_MKWRITE,     LPROCFS_TYPE_LATENCY,   "page_mkwrite" },

What are you looking for?
